fields.name: Anxiety Disorders
fields.definition: A group of mental health conditions characterized by excessive, persistent fear or worry that is difficult to control and interferes significantly with daily life. Unlike the normal anxiety people feel before an exam or job interview, anxiety disorders involve disproportionate dread that does not go away and can worsen over time. Research shows that one in three adults will have an anxiety disorder in their lifetime (CAMH/Bandelow & Michaelis, 2015). They are the most common class of mental health conditions, affecting one in ten Canadians at any given time. Anxiety disorders are more common in women and are second only to substance use disorders in men.
fields.example: A person with an anxiety disorder may avoid driving on highways, calling strangers on the phone, or attending social events, even when they logically know the threat is minimal.

fields.name: Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD)
fields.definition: An anxiety disorder characterized by excessive, hard-to-control worry about a wide variety of topics, including work, health, family, and everyday matters, occurring more days than not for at least six months. The worry is out of proportion to the actual likelihood or impact of the anticipated events. CAMH notes that GAD patients may express excessive worries about losing their job or about the health and welfare of family members. Physical symptoms include muscle tension, fatigue, irritability, difficulty concentrating, and sleep disturbance. NIMH notes that GAD is among the most commonly seen anxiety disorders in primary care settings.
fields.example: Persistently worrying about whether family members are safe, whether finances are secure, and whether work performance is adequate, even when no actual problems exist.

fields.name: Panic Disorder
fields.definition: An anxiety disorder characterized by recurrent, unexpected panic attacks: sudden surges of intense fear or discomfort that peak within minutes. Symptoms include heart palpitations, sweating, trembling, shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, and fear of losing control or dying. CAMH describes panic disorder as often accompanied by agoraphobia, and notes that patients experience persistent worry about having further attacks and avoidance of situations expected to bring on symptoms. To be diagnosed, a person must have ongoing concern about future attacks or make significant behavioral changes to prevent them.
fields.example: A person who has experienced several unexpected panic attacks begins avoiding public spaces, fearing another attack will occur with no way to escape.

fields.name: Social Anxiety Disorder
fields.definition: An anxiety disorder involving a fear or anxiety about being humiliated or scrutinized in social situations, lasting at least six months. CAMH notes that fears may be associated with social interactions, being observed, or performing, and may be limited to certain interactions or generalized to almost all of them. The fear causes significant distress or impairment in day-to-day functioning. Physical symptoms such as blushing, sweating, trembling, and nausea are common in feared situations. NIMH emphasizes that it goes far beyond ordinary shyness and can interfere with work, school, and relationships.
fields.example: Avoiding work presentations entirely, eating alone to avoid being watched, or rehearsing conversations for hours due to fear of saying something embarrassing.

fields.name: Specific Phobia
fields.definition: An anxiety disorder involving intense, irrational fear of a specific object or situation that poses little or no actual danger. The fear is out of proportion to the real risk, and the person typically recognizes this but cannot control the reaction. Common phobias include fear of heights, flying, animals, injections, or blood. Exposure to the feared stimulus provokes immediate anxiety, and the person goes to great lengths to avoid it. NIMH notes that specific phobias are among the most common anxiety disorders and often begin in childhood. Diagnosis requires that the avoidance or distress significantly interferes with the person's routine.
fields.example: A person with a phobia of needles avoids all medical appointments, even when seriously ill, because the possibility of an injection causes overwhelming panic.

fields.body: Diagnosis requires that symptoms be considered excessive and unreasonable, cause significant distress or impairment in daily functioning, and persist for six months or more. The clinician's task is to match symptoms to the appropriate anxiety disorder, since patients often have more than one. CAMH recommends prioritizing the most distressing disorder to guide the treatment plan. Screening tools such as the GAD-7 (for general anxiety) and PHQ-9 (for co-occurring depression) help assess severity. Other psychiatric disorders must be ruled out: adjustment disorder, depression with anxious features, substance-induced anxiety, and personality disorders can all present with anxiety symptoms. Physical causes such as thyroid disease should also be excluded.
fields.name: How Anxiety Disorders Are Diagnosed
fields.example: A patient presents with racing thoughts, trouble sleeping, and avoidance of social situations. The clinician uses the GAD-7 and a clinical interview to determine whether the primary issue is generalized anxiety, social anxiety, or both.

fields.name: Cognitive-Behavioural Therapy (CBT) for Anxiety
fields.definition: The most effective form of psychotherapy for anxiety disorders, according to both CAMH and NIMH. CBT helps people identify and change unhelpful thinking patterns and behaviors that maintain anxiety. It teaches practical skills to manage anxious thoughts, gradually face feared situations (exposure), and develop healthier responses to anxiety triggers. CAMH notes that while many psychological treatments (relaxation training, meditation, biofeedback, stress management) can help, CBT has the strongest research support. Treatment may also include supportive counselling or family therapy. Early intervention improves outcomes significantly.
fields.example: A person with social anxiety works with a therapist to challenge the belief that others are constantly judging them, then gradually practices entering social situations they previously avoided.

fields.name: Medication for Anxiety Disorders
fields.definition: Antidepressants (particularly SSRIs and SNRIs) are the most commonly prescribed medications for anxiety disorders. They work by affecting neurotransmitter systems involved in the stress response. Anti-anxiety medications called benzodiazepines may provide short-term relief but carry risks of dependence. CAMH advises clinicians to be familiar with both antidepressants and anxiolytics, and to help patients make informed treatment choices between medication and psychotherapy. NIMH notes that medications do not cure anxiety disorders but can significantly reduce symptoms. Treatment plans often combine medication with psychotherapy for the best outcomes.
fields.example: A person with panic disorder starts an SSRI under medical supervision, experiencing gradual reduction in panic attack frequency over several weeks, while simultaneously beginning CBT.

fields.question: How common is it for anxiety disorders to co-occur with other conditions?
fields.answer: Very common. CAMH notes that anxiety is frequently comorbid with depression, and that the combination of depression and severe anxiety increases the risk of suicidality. DSM-5 includes a specifier for depressive disorders called 'with anxious distress' to capture this overlap. Anxiety disorders also commonly co-occur with substance use disorders, eating disorders, and other mental health conditions. CAMH clinicians emphasize that when multiple disorders are present, identifying and prioritizing each one is essential to effective treatment.

fields.question: When does normal anxiety become an anxiety disorder?
fields.answer: CAMH identifies several factors that distinguish clinical anxiety from everyday worry: the degree of distress caused by the symptoms, the effect on the person's ability to work, study, socialize, and manage daily tasks, and whether the anxiety is disproportionate to the actual situation. Anxiety disorders may make people feel anxious most of the time or cause brief intense episodes for no clear reason. People with anxiety disorders are usually aware that their fears are unreasonable but cannot stop them. The anxiety can be viewed on a continuum from mild uneasiness to panic, and from occasional distress to constant unease.

fields.question: How common are anxiety disorders?
fields.answer: Anxiety disorders are the most common class of mental health conditions. CAMH reports that one in ten Canadians is affected at any given time and that one in three adults will experience an anxiety disorder in their lifetime. NIMH data shows that an estimated 31.1% of U.S. adults experience an anxiety disorder at some point. Anxiety disorders are more common in women than men. Despite being highly treatable, people often live with anxiety disorders for years before receiving a diagnosis.

fields.question: What is the biological purpose of anxiety?
fields.answer: Anxiety is the body's built-in alarm system, often called the 'fight or flight' response. It is essential for survival, activating when we perceive a threat or danger so that we either confront it or escape from it. CAMH describes normal anxiety as something everyone feels before important events like exams or job interviews. The problem arises when this alarm system fires too easily, too intensely, or stays on long after the threat has passed. In anxiety disorders, the brain's threat detection circuitry is essentially misfiring, producing an alarm response to situations that pose little or no real danger.

fields.question: What self-help strategies can support recovery from anxiety disorders?
fields.answer: CAMH recommends several strategies alongside professional treatment: developing a social support network of family, friends, and support groups who can help recognize triggers; eating a healthy diet, sleeping well, and exercising regularly; becoming more aware of the present moment through yoga and mindfulness practices; learning new skills and activities to build confidence; using faith, religion, or spiritual practices if they are meaningful to you; drinking alcohol only in moderation if at all; and including your partner in your recovery. CAMH emphasizes that recovery can be a long process and that professional treatment combined with self-help produces the best outcomes.

fields.question: Can people with anxiety disorders just 'calm down' or 'stop worrying' if they try hard enough?
fields.answer: No. CAMH notes that people with anxiety disorders are usually aware that their fears are irrational and excessive, yet cannot stop them. Many describe the experience as knowing their fears are unreasonable but being unable to control them. Anxiety disorders involve real changes in brain function and the body's stress response system. They are medical conditions that respond to evidence-based treatments such as CBT and medication, not willpower. Telling someone with an anxiety disorder to 'just relax' is like telling someone with a broken leg to 'just walk it off.'
